name: Bright Data Browser API (formerly Scraping Browser)
maintenance_status: Active. Bright Data now documents the product as Browser API, with current Playwright/Puppeteer/Selenium, session API, custom CDP, CLI, security, and pricing documentation maintained through 2025-2026.
license: Managed commercial service under Bright Data's license agreement: limited, revocable, non-transferable use for the client's internal business operations, with no right to sublicense. It can be called by AGPL-3.0 ApplyPilot without relicensing ApplyPilot, but service restrictions, compliance review, data terms, and target-site permissions apply.
pricing_model: Bandwidth-based. Pay as you go is $8/GB. Current monthly options list 71 GB for $499 at $7/GB, 166 GB for $999 at $6/GB, and 399 GB for $1,999 at $5/GB; enterprise is custom. Managed browsers, proxy selection, JavaScript rendering, CAPTCHA solving, and unlocking are presented as included features.
marginal_cost_per_attempt: [uncertain] Cost depends on downloaded bytes, not browser minutes. At $8/GB, a 10-50 MB application flow costs about $0.08-$0.40; at $5-$7/GB it costs about $0.05-$0.35. Images, fonts, video, ads, retries, CAPTCHA, and multi-page flows can increase bytes; blocking unnecessary resources can reduce bandwidth. Measure provider-reported bandwidth per session.
projected_cost_per_verified_apply: [uncertain] ApplyPilot's 2026-07-09 baseline is 909 verified applies across 6,371 terminal attempts at $2.0405 all-in. If every attempt added 25 MB at $8/GB, the illustrative $0.20 per attempt would mechanically add about $1.40 per success at the unchanged 14.27% rate, producing roughly $3.44 all-in. Bright Data is economical only if narrowly routed to cases where unlocking materially raises verified completion or avoids a more expensive fallback.
billing_granularity: Metered by Browser API traffic in GB under pay-as-you-go or included monthly GB. The public pricing page does not describe browser-minute, idle-time, or per-session minimum charges. Resource blocking and browser cache can reduce traffic. Exact byte accounting, failed-session billing, and rounding should be verified in an account before canarying. [uncertain]
model_cost: Browser API controlled directly by Playwright, Puppeteer, or Selenium requires no LLM. Any ApplyPilot semantic planner, answerer, verifier, or separate Bright Data agent/MCP feature adds model cost outside the Browser API bandwidth price.
infrastructure_cost: Bright Data supplies remote GUI Chromium, proxy networks, fingerprint/header/session management, CAPTCHA detection/solving, retries, scaling, and unblocking. These are bundled into bandwidth pricing; ApplyPilot still pays its controller, model, evidence storage, and operator costs.
human_recovery_cost: [uncertain] A local Browser API Debugger and session inspection can help development, but reviewed product docs do not establish an end-user Live View takeover and OTP handoff workflow for production applications. Authentication or ambiguous final states may therefore require a custom control surface or fallback to ApplyPilot's owner browser.
adapter_maintenance_tco: [uncertain] Medium. Standard CDP and Selenium reduce code migration, and managed unblocking can reduce anti-block repair. ApplyPilot still owns ATS selectors, answer provenance, exactly-once submission, data minimization, bandwidth optimization, session restrictions, and compliance review. The one-initial-navigation model may require flow redesign.
verified_completion_rate: [uncertain] Bright Data publishes no ATS-stratified, positively verified job-application completion rate. Unblocking and CAPTCHA claims concern page access, not correct application submission. Benchmark against the 14.27% mixed ApplyPilot control only with identical evidence rules.
failure_modes: Expired, duplicate, ineligible, or policy-denied jobs remain invalid work.
Compliance review, KYC, target restrictions, or account suspension can prevent service use.
WebSocket/CDP/HTTPS disconnect, hosted browser failure, timeout, or retry exhaustion can interrupt a run.
One initial navigation per Browser API session can conflict with cross-origin or multi-site application flows.
Login, MFA, OTP, account ownership, or unsupported CAPTCHA can still block completion.
Unsupported fields, uploads, dialogs, or ambiguous final confirmation can produce false success.
crash_recovery: [uncertain] Bright Data documents intelligent retries/session recovery and an API listing active/historical sessions with status, URL, navigations, duration, CAPTCHA, bandwidth, and errors. It does not provide ApplyPilot-level deterministic resume or exactly-once semantics after a crash near final submit.
exactly_once_submit_safety: Not native. ApplyPilot must preserve dedup keys, pre-submit checkpoints, one final action, response/DOM capture, no blind retry after ambiguity, and delayed email/ATS reconciliation. Session metadata is useful evidence but not a transaction log.
verification_evidence_tier: Browser control can capture ATS IDs or response data when exposed, confirmation DOM/URL, screenshots, cookies, network information, and session metadata including status, duration, CAPTCHA, bandwidth, and errors. Email remains external; session success alone is not verified application evidence.
verification_latency: [uncertain] DOM/network and session metadata can be available synchronously, while email/account evidence is delayed. Automated unblocking/retries can extend attempt time. Measure navigation, CAPTCHA, submission, session-finalization, and reconciliation timestamps separately.
headless_headed_parity: [uncertain] Bright Data states Browser API runs GUI/headful Chromium on its infrastructure while developers control it as a headless remote API. That may improve parity relative to headless Chrome, but it still differs from the user's local profile, extensions, hardware, IP, and account history; no ATS parity benchmark is published.
python_integration: Good. Official examples support Python Playwright and Selenium. Python Playwright connects with chromium.connect_over_cdp to wss://brd.superproxy.io:9222; Selenium uses an HTTPS remote endpoint on port 9515.
playwright_cdp_reuse: Good for existing Playwright action logic through connect_over_cdp. It does not reuse ApplyPilot's local CDP port or local Chrome profile in place. Bright Data requires its zone credentials and warns against adding unsupported connection options.
migration_effort: Medium. A basic provider launcher is small, but production integration needs zone credentials, bandwidth/cost telemetry, resource blocking, one-navigation flow testing, owner-auth fallback, compliance approval, session/error joins, and evidence capture before exactly-once submission.
ats_fit: [uncertain] Potentially useful for protected long-tail or browser-heavy hosts where legitimate automated access is permitted and local/CDP routes fail due to blocking. It is likely unnecessary and expensive for stable Ashby/Greenhouse deterministic adapters, and one-initial-navigation/session plus auth constraints may impair Workday or cross-domain flows.
deterministic_coverage: [uncertain] The remote browser should execute Chromium selectors, but the service does not add ATS schema knowledge. It may recover access failures through managed proxies/CAPTCHA and retries; deterministic field coverage remains controlled by ApplyPilot adapters.
escape_rate: [uncertain] Unknown. Managed unblocking may reduce challenge escapes, but authentication, OTP, sensitive questions, one-navigation limits, unsupported widgets, policy restrictions, and ambiguous confirmations still require local, agent, or human fallback.
control_plane_overhead: Adds a remote CDP or Selenium connection, zone authentication, cross-network action round trips, managed retry latency, and session bandwidth accounting. Browser caching within a session can reduce repeated downloads; each session's navigation constraints must be reflected in adapter flow design.
observability_joinability: Moderate to good if ApplyPilot stores Bright Data session ID and zone with job, attempt, worker, and route IDs. The sessions API returns target/end URL, navigations, timestamps, duration, CAPTCHA state, bandwidth, status, and errors. Full DOM/network replay must be captured by ApplyPilot tooling.
persistent_profiles: [uncertain] Custom CDP Proxy.useSession preserves proxy-peer continuity across sessions, and cookies can be controlled during a session, but this is not evidence of durable portable Chrome user-data profiles. Do not assume local profile import, durable cookie persistence, or concurrent clone safety without a dedicated test.
existing_session_attach: Automation attaches to Bright Data-created cloud browsers by WebSocket/HTTPS credentials. It cannot safely attach to an existing authenticated local ApplyPilot Chrome tab/profile; retain the owner/local route for that requirement.
login_and_otp: [uncertain] Browser API can interact with login pages and authenticated sessions, but reviewed docs do not define production owner takeover, mailbox OTP relay, or secure local credential handoff. ApplyPilot should avoid transmitting owner credentials until data processing, retention, and control paths are approved.
extension_support: [uncertain] No supported customer Chrome-extension loading or native-messaging workflow was confirmed in the reviewed Browser API documentation. Treat extensions as unsupported unless Bright Data confirms it contractually and a canary verifies behavior.
session_data_boundary: Page traffic, credentials entered into pages, cookies, resumes, screenshots, and session metadata traverse Bright Data-managed browsers and proxy infrastructure. The security platform lists ISO 27001/27017/27018 and SOC reporting, but the current license states Bright Data may retain client-collected data and use it for its own purposes in its sole discretion. This is a material concern for candidate PII and requires legal/DPA clarification before production use.
required_field_completeness: Not native. ApplyPilot adapters must enumerate required controls, validation messages, conditional fields, and upload completion, then refuse incomplete plans before final submit.
answer_provenance: Not native. ApplyPilot must derive answers only from approved resume/profile/saved-answer evidence and log source IDs. Managed browser/unblocking features do not validate candidate claims.
unsupported_question_fallback: Unknown, sensitive, or free-text questions should stop the route and return a structured fallback to ApplyPilot's verified answerer or human queue. Automated unblocking must never imply permission to answer or submit.
prompt_injection_resistance: Bright Data advises treating web content as untrusted, validating/filtering it before LLM use, preferring structured extraction, storing credentials securely, and scoping API permissions. ApplyPilot must additionally enforce domain allowlists, secret isolation, limited tools, hostile-page tests, and a hard submit boundary.
site_permission_and_terms: High scrutiny required. Bright Data requires lawful use, acceptable-use compliance, and sometimes KYC/compliance review, and may suspend uses that create security, liability, or blocking risk. ApplyPilot must separately establish target ATS permission and must not use unblocking, residential proxies, fingerprinting, or CAPTCHA solving to evade access controls.
irreversible_action_guard: No application-domain guard is built in. ApplyPilot must require complete approved answers, dedup and policy checks, a pre-submit checkpoint, exactly one final action, and authoritative confirmation; retries after an ambiguous result must quarantine rather than resubmit.
concurrency: The pricing page advertises unlimited concurrent requests as included, but practical limits may be account-, zone-, compliance-, target-, or contract-specific. Confirm session creation and simultaneous-browser limits in the account before capacity planning. [uncertain]
startup_latency: [uncertain] Official docs say users connect to managed cloud Chrome but provide no reviewed cold-start SLA. Measure WebSocket/HTTPS attach, first navigation, CAPTCHA/unblocking time, and warm-session response from ApplyPilot workers.
warm_session_reuse: Partial. Browser caching can accelerate repeated same-domain activity during a session, CLI named sessions keep a connection open, and Proxy.useSession can preserve proxy-peer continuity. The FAQ says each Browser API session supports only one initial navigation, although interactions and resulting navigations can continue; durable browser-profile reuse is not established.
tracing_and_replay: Moderate. Browser API Debugger provides Chrome DevTools-style live inspection, session APIs expose metadata, and Playwright/Selenium can capture screenshots and network details. A managed video/DOM replay and deterministic replay guarantee was not confirmed in the reviewed docs. [uncertain]
deployment_modes: Managed Bright Data Browser API accessed by WebSocket for Playwright/Puppeteer or HTTPS for Selenium, plus local client scripts and Bright Data CLI. No self-hosted Browser API deployment was identified.
browser_version_matrix: Managed GUI Chromium/Chrome optimized for scraping, with Playwright, Puppeteer, and Selenium compatibility. No Firefox, WebKit, or WebDriver BiDi support was confirmed in reviewed primary docs.
canary_stop_loss: Use zone/account spend controls, explicit browser close, bandwidth reporting per session, resource blocking, max duration, max retries, and concurrency caps. ApplyPilot must add route allowlists, cost-per-attempt ceilings, failure breakers, and immediate stop on policy/compliance or ambiguous-submit signals.
benchmark_design: Use only approved targets. Compare the current local route with Bright Data on a matched protected-host slice, not the full fleet. Record provider session ID, bytes, plan rate, target/end URL, duration, CAPTCHA, retries, evidence tier, completion, fallback, and human minutes; include a no-images/ad-block arm to estimate bandwidth sensitivity.
route_funnel: Eligibility, permission, and privacy review -> deterministic local route first -> Bright Data canary only for approved access-failure bucket -> minimal-data session -> required-field/provenance checks -> pre-submit guard -> one submit -> DOM/network evidence -> email reconciliation -> verified, ambiguous quarantine, fallback, or reject.
step_context_budget: [uncertain] Direct deterministic control needs no model tokens but must meter actions, CDP/Selenium round trips, navigations, screenshots, and especially downloaded bytes by resource type. Any semantic agent must separately meter DOM/accessibility or screenshot context and model tokens.
cache_replay_yield: [uncertain] Browser cache and same proxy session can reduce repeated asset downloads and setup, but no ApplyPilot warm-hit, invalidation, or no-model replay rate exists. One-navigation/session behavior and identity changes may limit reuse.
confidence_intervals: [uncertain] No job-application intervals exist. Use Wilson intervals for verified completion and fallback, bootstrap cost per verified apply, and byte-distribution percentiles; start with at least 30-50 eligible attempts in each approved host bucket and include zero-success stopping rules.
historical_bucket_impact: [uncertain] ApplyPilot's fresh history includes 1,894 agent/browser-runtime failures costing $310.2143 and 2,042 preflight/policy failures costing $289.3490, with Workday at $14.6340 and other/long-tail at $3.1846 per apply. Bright Data should target only the subset proven to be access/challenge failures. It cannot reduce expired/policy work, and bandwidth cost can worsen economics if routed broadly.
recommendation: Reject as a general ApplyPilot browser route; consider a tightly controlled watchlist experiment only for lawful, approved access/challenge failures. Bandwidth pricing is materially higher than browser-hour competitors for media-heavy application pages, and the license's broad collected-data retention/use clause is a serious candidate-PII concern requiring legal clarification before any real-profile test.
